---
title: "On Scientific Writing and Blogs"
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2024-09-14"
categories: [news, blog, science, collaboration]
image: "Freud_Hysterie.png"
draft: True
---

## Scientific Writing

The first scientific journal was established by the Royal Society in 1665 (see the [Philosophical Transactions on JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/journal/philtran1665167). Today, publications <!--#in peer-reviewed journals--> are the most valuable currency in science. I guess it is with good reason. Publishers made it possible for the community to distribute knowledge. It is a way to communicate and get feedback. The speed at which it was and is distributed is rather slow, and there are different reasons for that. Nevertheless, progress in the sciences around the world would have been way slower without those printed journals.

Today, the big publishing houses get some bad press [@editorialGuardianViewAcademic2019]. Mostly, because publishers make a lot of money without even having a real product. It sounds like a scam if you hear this for the first time.

## How does the publication system work?

The journal's role is to disseminate knowledge and achievements made worldwide. Because in *science*, the only way to make research count is to make it public. Publication is not just a way to reach fame. On the contrary, it embodies the values of the Enlightenment by opening up for criticism and embracing fallibility.

Publication could be any way of writing it down or discussing theory, explanation, and empirical evidence. Practically, the only way to make it count as an academic achievement is publication in a peer-reviewed scientific journal. There are other ways, like books, conference contributions, patents, preprints, or blogs, but they are rare and considered less prestigious.

When publishing a research paper, researchers submit their work in the hope of getting published as swiftly as possible. Journals also want to publish quickly and as much as possible, but they would also like to maintain qualitative standards. First, the journal editor decides whether the manuscript is worth a more thorough review. Editors have a key role in separating the wheat from the chaff. I can only imagine how much work it is to go through all the manuscripts handed in. Criteria for desk rejection could include the topic not fitting the journal or obvious scientific shortcomings that cannot be fixed. <!--# Editor Miyakawa, 2020. -->

Even within fields, the number of registered journals can be immense. [ELSEVIER](https://www.elsevier.com/products/journals?query=&page=1&subjectArea=health&sortBy=relevance) alone currently holds 1825 health-related journals (07-07-2026). So individual journals try to develop unique selling points. Many explicitly ask for *novel topics and insights*. This means that repeating experiments is hard to sell. This is not true for all fields. We recently saw that in physics with (another) seemingly fraudulent attempt to create superconductors. Labs around the world tried to replicate the authors' experiments without success. People seem to debate if that many replications were necessary in that particular case. It seemed unlikely to begin with. Some red flags already indicated that something was wrong. Nevertheless, replications are an important part of science. They are the equivalent of measure twice, cut once. But replications don't sell. So many journals are reluctant to accept them. The editor has to make sure that the journal lives by its standards and sells. In the background, the publisher wants as many papers as possible published in their journal. Again, there are no costs involved in doing so. So depending on the journal's agenda, the editor might get some economic outlines on what to accept, too. You could only hope that the editor is well paid by the publisher, right? In some cases, editors might get some compensation. But in many journals, editors are academics getting paid by the university and playing editor in their spare time.

Let's say that the editor accepts a manuscript, judging it worthy of a more thorough inspection. This is when the peer review process starts. \[Nature\](<https://www-nature-com.uaccess.univie.ac.at/>) was the first journal to install systematic peer review for all handed in manuscripts.

-   The idea behind peer-review

-   Famous story of Einstein not wanted peer-review

-   No-one gets paid

Their products (papers) are written by the scientists who are mostly payed by universities (mostly taxes). The editors of the journals and the peers who review the papers are also volunteers from within academia. They do not earn any extra credits for this work. Especially the peer review is entirely out of good-will as they are mostly anonymous. Some journals keep a list of all peers which is publicly available. But nobody cares about that. When the peer-review is completed and the paper is ready for publication, the real fraud starts. Publishing itself often costs money. If you want everybody in the world being able to access your paper, publication might cost your institute 10.000€ or more (Nature, access [@APCCellJournal; @PublishingOptionsNature]! On the other hand, the more traditional way is to let the reader pay instead of the authors. Universities around the world pay horrendous amounts of money to be able to access articles that their employees write! In some cases both parties pay - the readers and the authors. To be fair, things are changing. Open access journals getting more popular. In this system the authors pay once and readers can access the article for free. Proponents of this system argue that scientific results are a popular good and should be accessible by everyone around the world. On the other hand, this system does privilege richer research groups, while scientists in less economically strong areas of the world have a hard time to publish findings. My grandfather used to say that things that do not cost anything are worth nothing. Which is another critique for open access publication. As journals earn money be publishing, not reading, the quality of the articles is less important, leading to many crappy publications in those journals. Some might think that taking the economic part out of the equation all together is the way to go. So called Diamond open access journals have no publication fee and articles are free to read. It sounds like this is what we should strive for. Currently, this is definitely more of an exception than the norm but it could grow stronger. Don't forget that this does not mean that there is no money involved. Researchers, editors and peer-reviewers still get payed. But they get paid anyways by the universities or companies directly. It would only mean to take out the middle human. Personally, I am still skeptical. I suggest comparing the short history of podcasts (or social media, if you like) with the push towards "free science". While most podcasts are free to listen to, there is a lot of money involved. I really hope that we do not see more commertial adds in scientific journals (in addition to official funding of reserach by the industry).

# They should just...

The academic community seems to be frustrated with the current situation. Nearly every scientist I know regularly drops complaints or self-aware jokes about the current system. There is a lot of discussion online and everybody seems to know what *Elsevier*, *Springer* and *Wiley*, the universities or funding agencies should do, to make the world a better place. The situation reminds me of each soccer world-cup during which everyone becomes a sports analyst and coach, knowing exactly what to do.

Today I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?\
Maybe not much. Because your publication list *is* what makes you a successful scientist. I doubt that the system will change overnight. Still, we should at least attempt to have a positive influence on the situation. Being patient is a great value with surprising research results and so it is with complex systems like science itself.
